explanation_md: `m_size` is initialized to `Size::Medium`. `getSize()` returns that value. The comparison `b.getSize() == Box::Size::Medium` is true, so `"medium"` is printed. Note that outside the class, the enum must be qualified as `Box::Size::Medium`.

prompt_md: Outside a class named `Ship`, how do you refer to a nested struct named `Cargo`?

options.b: `Ship.Cargo` — use dot notation like accessing a member

explanation_md: Nested types are scoped to the enclosing class, so they must be accessed via the scope resolution operator `::`. `Ship::Cargo` is the correct qualified name. Dot notation is for object member access, not type names.

prompt_md: A nested class defined in the `private` section of an outer class is:

options.a: Accessible to all code that includes the header
options.b: Accessible only inside the outer class's member functions
options.c: Automatically made `public` because it is a type, not a variable
options.d: Accessible to subclasses but not to other external code

explanation_md: Nested types follow the same access control rules as data members. A `private` nested type can only be named by the outer class's own member functions. Code outside the class — even code in the same file — cannot refer to the type by name.

options.b: `Mode::Off` is not in scope outside the class; it should be `Lamp::Mode::Off`
options.c: Enum class values cannot be used in `if` conditions
options.d: The initializer `{Mode::Off}` is not valid for an enum class member

explanation_md: Inside `main`, the name `Mode` is not visible directly — it is scoped to `Lamp`. The comparison must use the fully qualified name `Lamp::Mode::Off`. The class and its initializer are fine; the bug is purely a missing qualifier on the outside usage.

prompt_md: When should you prefer a nested type over a type defined in the global namespace?

options.a: Always — all types should be nested inside a class for safety
options.b: When the type is tightly coupled to one class and would be meaningless without it
options.c: When the type needs to be used in more than two different classes
options.d: Only when the type is a `struct`, not an `enum class`

explanation_md: Nesting is appropriate when the type only makes sense as part of one class — its name conveys the coupling, and the qualified name `ClassName::TypeName` reads naturally. Types used across many unrelated classes should live in the broader namespace or their own header to avoid artificial dependencies.
